utterances: I think one thing that the progressives were upset about, that during that process of review, the option of withdrawing immediately, or at least putting that on the table, was rejected out of hand.
It was never actually considered, according to the president and his advisors.
They never put that on the table, and I think there was a concern that the views of people who would like to see a withdrawal were never accurately reflected or given the attention they deserved.
All right, Nate, thanks very much for the call.
Let's see if we can go next to - this is Daniel(ph), Daniel with us from Santa Rosa, California.
Hi.
I am a progressive, and I would like to say I disagree with what your people just said a minute ago.
I feel that I was lied to over this whole withdrawal.
Obama put himself right next to John Kerry in saying he was going to take on the whole Afghan problem as a police action.
We look at that as a complete drawdown in the war.
Now the people in the Edwards camp and everybody told us all along that Hillary Clinton and Obama were being - how about this - disingenuous to us.
Well, that was about the war in Iraq.
There were no talks about Afghanistan.
No, no, no, no, no, no, no. That was also about Afghanistan.
And I can look right now and see New York Times articles where Obama came out and said he supported John Kerry's idea of treating even Afghanistan and going after Osama bin Laden as a police action.
I think that was in terms of prosecution rather than prosecution in the war.
Yes.
Yes.
And that is where we're angry.
So we - to come back out with this - oh, I don't know the words to use.
But to come out with this disingenuous argument that Obama never said this.
Well, how about this.
I was solicited, number of times - because I give to the Democratic Party and will volunteer for the Democratic Party and have worked on campaigns for the Democratic Party.
But I was solicited time and time again and told by Obama people that he was going to stop the wars, period.
All right.
Daniel, thanks very much for the call.
Appreciate it.
Let's go next to - this is Melba(ph), Melba in Charlotte.
Hi.
I couldn't disagree with Daniel more.
Obama always said he was going to escalate in Afghanistan, and I don't understand where these people are coming away with this from.
I mean, they voted for him, and I volunteered for Obama and I worked for Obama and he always talked about getting out of Iraq and going, you know, more into Afghanistan.
Well, that's more my memory of it as well.
Okay.
That's it.
I support the president and what he's doing because he campaigned on it.
All right, Melba.
Thanks very much for the call.
Email from Mary(ph) in Iowa City.
Many of the peace advocates in Iowa City did not support Obama.
I'm an anti-war supporter who did.
Unfortunately, Obama did not speak to those people who are anti-war and did not vote for him so he would never be able to convince these hard left progressives.
And this is from Donna(ph) in Medford, Washington.
I was against the war from the start.
I am part of the progressive base, but I voted for President Obama because I trust his judgment more than anything.
I think we need someone with good judgment who knows all the facts, deciding what we should be doing.
I wish Congress would get behind the president.
And, Ken, this is not an issue that's going to go away.
No, not at all.

And, of course, it's the Republicans who are probably enjoying this the most because they know that for the Obama policy on Afghanistan to succeed, it's going to have to be the Republicans' support because we're seeing more and more Democrats who feel that they were betrayed, even though, as we've correctly said, Obama has campaigned on the fact that they - that Afghanistan is the good war.
But more and more Democrats in the House and the Senate are very nervous about the escalation.
And, Faiz Shakir, do you think those Democrats in the House and Senate are going to be pressing for an early vote up or down on Afghanistan?
Well, I think the majority of Democrats, both in the House and the Senate, are going to end up supporting the president's policy, but it's going to come at a cost.
I think that Congressman Obey's proposal to institute a war tax might actually end up gaining some favor.

I think people are going to want to see that there are some costs inflicted, that the American people understand the costs that are inflicted with this war and that cost is shared politically as well across the board.
Although Nancy Pelosi, the speaker, dismissed that out of hand.
Right.
She did.
But I think that there'll be more support from it in different places.
Faiz Shakir, thank you very much for your time today.
Appreciate it.
Thank you.
Faiz Shakir, research director at the Center for American Progress and editor in chief of thinkprogress.org and the Progress Report, who was kind enough to join us here in the Studio 3A.
You're listening to TALK OF THE NATION and the Political Junkie from NPR News.

Traditional solutions may be more common in Afghanistan than formal legal solutions.
A girl from the perpetrator's family is given in marriage to the offended family, the rapist takes his victim as a wife - sometimes as a second wife.
But a strange turn of events may have won Gulnaz a chance at a different ending.
A group of filmmakers, commissioned by the European Union, interviewed Gulnaz and other prisoners at length, hoping to bring attention to their plight.
Filmmaker Leslie Knott was involved in the project.
Finally, we came across a few women who were really, really willing to want to tell their story, and really passionate about sharing what they had gone through.
And the reason for that was because they wanted to make sure that it would never happen again to women in Afghanistan.
But after viewing the finished product, E.U.
diplomats decided not to release the film they had funded.
Vygaudus Usackas, the special representative of the E.U.
in Afghanistan, says he wants publicity for the situation of Afghan women, but fears that Gulnaz and other women in the film may be put in danger.
I'm in favor for the broadest publicity possible.
But at the same time, the sole responsibility of myself, as representative of European Union, is to ensure that there are no repercussions to the safety and well being of women.
Gulnaz says she wants her story told but not inside Afghanistan.
Usackas says that it's impossible to prevent a film from reaching Afghanistan via YouTube or Facebook, which could put Gulnaz in danger.
But a letter sent to the filmmakers from an E.U.
representative stated the E.U.
delegation also has to consider its relations with the justice institutions, in connection with the other work that it is doing in the sector.
Usackas says that line in the letter does not represent E.U.
policy.
But advocates here suggests that the E.U.
doesn't want to sour relations with the Afghan government over the issue of women's rights.
In any case, the controversy and media attention appear to have helped Gulnaz get a reduced sentence, and she may find a home in one of Afghanistan's anonymous women's shelters.
But Badem Bagh prison is still full of women with similar stories.
(Foreign language spoken)
Meena is a former Afghan government employee.
She asked to give only one name.
She says she recently got into a fight with her husband.
He threw boiling water on her, and she has scars on her face and arms.
The police arrested both of them and Meena says she expects a lengthy sentence.
(Foreign language spoken)
The media should pay attention, our voice should be heard, says Meena.
But she's concerned there will be less attention as the international presence in Afghanistan is drawing down.
The United Nations recently warned in a report that time is running out to push Afghanistan's government to follow its own laws on violence against women.
Georgette Gagnon directs the U.N.
human rights office in Afghanistan.
We're worried that women's rights will be compromised and there will be retrograde steps taken.
Women themselves are very much counting on internationals to support them over the next few years.
And, in our view, it's now or never on this issue.
Gagnon says as things stand, Afghan laws are often trumped by traditional Afghan justice, which she says doesn't protect women and often lets perpetrators walk free.
Quil Lawrence, NPR News.
And we have one update since this report was filed.

The Afghan government has announced a pardon for Gulnaz, the main character in Quil's story, though it's not clear if, according to Afghan tradition, she will be forced to marry the man who raped her.

At some point this week, the road to economic recovery screeched to a halt at the edge of a cliff.

The IMF cut its forecast for global economic growth next year.
Europe is teetering closer to a possible banking collapse.

This week, the Federal Reserve announced a new plan to stimulate the economy.
They called it Operation Twist.
And what is it?
Here's Adam Davidson, host of NPR's PLANET MONEY.
The twist is a desperate attempt to make an incredibly boring monetary policy move sound a little more exciting, a little more active perhaps.
The Fed is constantly buying and selling U.S.
government bonds.
And in normal times, that's just a very routine, you know, uninteresting thing.
But obviously, we don't live in normal times.
And what they've decided to do is buy more 10-year, 30-year, more longer-dated bonds.

The idea being let's push down long-term interest rates so that mortgages will be low, credit cards interest rates will be low, companies that want to build factories will be able to borrow money even more cheaply so that they can build those factories, hire workers, get the economy going again.
That's the idea anyway.
Doesn't that make you want to fall asleep?
Yeah.
I don't understand how that's going to impact the economy.
Can you explain what the thinking behind it is?
Sure.
Now, first of all, I should say I don't think this is going to have a big impact on the economy.
I don't think anyone thinks this is going to have a big impact on the economy.
